emergency_contact.emergency_contact_relationship: Sister-in-law who lives 5 minutes away; she often watches the kids and is familiar with their routines, allergies, pediatrician, and preferred hospital.

health_conditions.health_conditions_line_1: Evan (age 11) has mild, well-controlled exercise-induced asthma. He carries an inhaler (albuterol) in his pool bag at all times; it is labeled with his name and instructions. Triggers include prolonged, intense swimming and very cold air. We have not had a serious episode in over two years, but if he seems short of breath, wheezing, or is coughing persistently after exertion, please have him sit out, use his inhaler, and call us immediately if there is no improvement within a few minutes.
health_conditions.health_conditions_line_2: Sophie (age 8) has a mild peanut allergy that typically presents as hives around the mouth and mild swelling if she accidentally ingests peanuts or peanut butter. She does NOT carry an EpiPen at this time because her allergist has classified her reactions as mild, but we still take it seriously. If she reports that she thinks she has eaten something with peanuts, please have her rinse her mouth with water, monitor for hives, swelling, or trouble breathing, and contact us right away so we can decide if she needs to be seen. We have instructed her not to share snacks with other children at the pool.
health_conditions.health_conditions_line_3: Laura (primary member) has no medical conditions that affect pool use, but she does occasionally experience low blood sugar if she has not eaten and has been swimming for a long time. If she appears lightheaded or faint, having her sit down in the shade with water and a small snack (she typically carries granola bars or fruit in her bag) is usually sufficient. No other chronic health issues or physical limitations are present for any family member that would impact safe participation in normal pool activities.
